title: Specimen: acid–base models and buffer response

objectives.statement: Identify conjugate acid–base pairs in a proton-transfer reaction and justify each label.
Choose a Brønsted–Lowry or Lewis model for a bounded reaction and state what the model explains.
Predict which buffer component consumes a small addition of strong acid and explain the resulting pH behaviour and its limits.

scenes.title: One reaction, two models
scenes.contentHtml:
One reaction, two models

Acid–base labels are tools for tracking a particular change. In this specimen, the first question is whether a proton moved; the second is whether an electron pair was donated and accepted.

Callout

This lesson teaches chemical models. It does not support personal interpretation of blood tests, symptoms, medicines, or treatment.

Before you begin

You should be able to draw a Lewis structure and identify a lone pair. If that feels fragile, follow the recovery route in the final scene before attempting the assessment.

Objectives

After the lesson, you should be able to identify conjugate pairs and choose the narrower model that answers a stated question.

scenes.title: Proton transfer and electron-pair acceptance
scenes.contentHtml:
Proton transfer and electron-pair acceptance

Brønsted–Lowry model

A Brønsted–Lowry acid donates a proton, and a Brønsted–Lowry base accepts a proton. When an acid loses a proton, the remaining species is its conjugate base. When a base gains a proton, the product is its conjugate acid.

In this reaction, water donates a proton and ammonia accepts it. The conjugate pairs are water/hydroxide and ammonium/ammonia. Pair members differ by one proton.

Lewis model

A Lewis base donates an electron pair, and a Lewis acid accepts an electron pair. Proton transfer fits this broader model because the base donates a pair toward the proton. Some Lewis reactions contain no transferable proton.

Evidence boundary

Source Note
claims claim-bronsted-model, claim-lewis-modelsources source-openstax-chemistry, source-iupac-gold-book

The claims ledger records the exact scope, confidence, and supporting sources for these definitions.

scenes.title: Deep reading: choosing the useful model
scenes.contentHtml:
Deep reading: choosing the useful model

This scene deliberately contains sustained prose. Guided view divides it at its level-two headings; Reading view restores the same source as a continuous section. The content is a non-production format specimen, not reviewed instruction.

A model answers a particular question

Chemical models are selective descriptions. They make some features of a reaction easier to track by leaving other features in the background. In a Brønsted–Lowry account, the central event is the transfer of a proton. The learner asks which species loses that proton, which species gains it, and how the products relate to the reactants. That account is especially useful when the task is to identify conjugate pairs or follow proton transfer through an equilibrium.

The Lewis account directs attention elsewhere. It asks where an electron pair begins, where that pair is accepted, and which bond is formed as a result. A proton-transfer reaction can be described this way because the accepting base supplies an electron pair to the proton. The same language also applies to reactions in which no proton moves. The models overlap, but they do not foreground the same evidence.

Choosing between them is therefore not a contest for the one permanently correct label. It is a decision about explanatory purpose. If a prompt asks for conjugate pairs, proton bookkeeping is the economical route. If it asks how a coordinate bond forms, tracking the electron pair is more revealing. A strong answer names the model and states what observable change that model is being used to explain.

Conjugate pairs are relationships, not isolated labels

The words acid and base can sound like permanent identities, but Brønsted–Lowry roles are assigned within a particular reaction direction. A species is called an acid when it donates a proton in the represented process and a base when it accepts one. The product left after donation is the conjugate base; the product formed after acceptance is the conjugate acid. Each conjugate pair therefore differs by one proton and, correspondingly, by one unit of charge.

This relational view prevents a common error: labelling every negatively charged species as a base and every positively charged species as an acid without comparing reactants and products. Charge is useful evidence, but it is not sufficient by itself. A learner should place the before-and-after formulas beside one another, identify the one-proton difference, and only then assign the roles. The comparison supplies a check that a memorized label cannot.

Reaction direction also matters. When an equilibrium is read in reverse, the donating and accepting species change. The two members of each conjugate pair do not change, but which member is acting as the acid or base in that direction does. This is why a complete explanation attaches every role to the represented transfer instead of listing labels without a mechanism.

Broader does not mean more informative

Because the Lewis definition includes proton-transfer cases and also covers electron-pair acceptance without proton transfer, it is often described as broader. Broader scope is useful, but it does not make the broader model the best answer to every question. A map of an entire country contains more territory than a street map; the street map may still be the better tool for finding a particular doorway. Models should be judged against the work they are being asked to do.

Suppose a learner must identify the conjugate acid of ammonia after proton acceptance. The Brønsted–Lowry model names the tracked particle and makes the ammonia/ammonium relationship explicit. Rephrasing the same event only as electron-pair donation is compatible with the chemistry, but it hides the one-proton comparison the task requires. Conversely, forcing proton-transfer language onto the formation of a Lewis adduct without a transferable proton would omit the relevant electron-pair interaction.

This principle extends beyond acid–base chemistry. Scientific explanations are bounded by scale, assumptions, and purpose. More general language can unify cases, while narrower language can expose a relation that matters for prediction or calculation. Expert model choice is not merely recalling definitions; it is matching a representation to the evidence and the requested conclusion.

Translate symbols into a causal sentence

Chemical equations compress many claims into a small space. A learner can manipulate the symbols correctly while remaining unable to explain what changed. To test understanding, translate the equation into a causal sentence: identify the donor, identify the acceptor, name the transferred proton or electron pair, and connect each product to its precursor. Then reverse the translation by checking that the verbal account predicts the displayed formulas and charges.

For a proton-transfer equation, one useful sequence is: compare species across the arrow; locate the one-proton difference; pair each reactant with its conjugate product; assign donor and acceptor roles; and check the charge change. For a Lewis interaction, locate an available electron pair, identify an electron-pair acceptor, and state which new bond or interaction the pair accounts for. Neither sequence replaces structural reasoning, solvent effects, equilibrium, or quantitative treatment. It simply makes the selected model explicit enough to inspect.

The final sentence should also state a limit. A donor–acceptor label does not by itself determine reaction extent, acid strength, rate, or biological consequence. Those questions require additional evidence and often another model. Stating the limit is part of using a model well: it marks the boundary between what the representation explains and what has not yet been established.

Evidence and reading route

Source Note
claims claim-bronsted-model, claim-lewis-modelsources source-openstax-chemistry, source-iupac-gold-book

The source records support the bounded introductory definitions used in this specimen. Read this scene in Guided mode to inspect four full text frames, then switch to Reading mode to compare the uninterrupted chapter rhythm.

scenes.title: Annotating conjugate pairs
scenes.contentHtml:
Annotating conjugate pairs

Question

For the reaction below, label the acid, base, conjugate acid, and conjugate base.

Worked Example
id worked-example-conjugate-pairs
  1. Compare reactants and products to locate the transferred proton.
  2. Acetate gains one proton, so it is the base; acetic acid is its conjugate acid.
  3. Water loses one proton, so it is the acid; hydroxide is its conjugate base.
  4. Check each pair: the formulas differ by exactly one proton and the charges differ by one unit.

Visual cross-check

Why the charge changes

Adding a proton adds positive charge; removing one removes positive charge. This bookkeeping check catches many reversed pair labels.

Evidence boundary

Source Note
claims claim-bronsted-modelsources source-openstax-chemistry, source-iupac-gold-book

The worked reasoning applies the sourced introductory Brønsted–Lowry definition. The claims ledger records its scope and review state.

scenes.title: Model-choice practice
scenes.contentHtml:
Model-choice practice

Try the set

Callout
id practice-pair-check

For each reaction, first identify the tracked change. Then label the relevant donor and acceptor and justify the model you chose.

  1. HCl + H2O -> H3O+ + Cl-
  2. BF3 + NH3 -> F3B-NH3
  3. NH4+ + H2O <=> NH3 + H3O+

Feedback path

  • If your pair members do not differ by one proton, redraw the before-and-after formulas side by side.
  • If no proton moves, ask which species accepts an electron pair rather than forcing Brønsted–Lowry labels.
  • If you chose a model but did not justify it, name the transferred particle or electron pair explicitly.

Common error

Misconception
id misconception-strong-charge

A negative charge does not automatically make a species a strong base, and a positive charge does not automatically make a species a strong acid. Structure, solvent, and equilibrium matter. Charge is useful bookkeeping, not a complete strength rule.

Evidence boundary

Source Note
claims claim-bronsted-model, claim-lewis-modelsources source-openstax-chemistry, source-iupac-gold-book

The practice set reuses the two sourced model definitions. It does not treat charge alone as a claim about acid or base strength.

scenes.title: Retrieval check
scenes.contentHtml:
Retrieval check

Close the earlier scenes before answering.

Recall without notes

Check
id retrieval-models
  1. Define a Brønsted–Lowry base in one sentence.
  2. State the relationship between an acid and its conjugate base.
  3. Give one reason the Lewis model is broader.
  4. Explain why NH4+ and NH3 can be a conjugate pair.

Calibration

Record confidence before checking the assessment logic. A high-confidence reversal of donor and acceptor should trigger the recovery route, not another immediate guess.

Evidence boundary

Source Note
claims claim-bronsted-model, claim-lewis-modelsources source-openstax-chemistry, source-iupac-gold-book

These retrieval prompts test the same bounded model definitions recorded in the claims ledger.

scenes.title: A buffer under perturbation
scenes.contentHtml:
A buffer under perturbation

Consider a closed teaching model containing a weak acid HA and its conjugate base A-. A small amount of strong acid adds protons to the model.

Make a prediction

Callout
id transfer-buffer-response

Predict which buffer component reacts with most of the added proton. Explain the response using both conjugate-pair language and a particle-level account. State one limit of the model.

Model response

The conjugate base consumes added proton to form the weak acid. This explains resistance to a sudden pH change; it does not mean pH is fixed or that buffer capacity is unlimited.

Evidence boundary

Source Note
claims claim-buffer-responsesources source-openstax-chemistry

The statement is scoped to a simplified chemical system, not a patient or biological diagnosis.

scenes.title: Synthesis, alternatives, and recovery
scenes.contentHtml:
Synthesis, alternatives, and recovery

Synthesis

Use the Brønsted–Lowry model to track proton donation and acceptance. Use the Lewis model when electron-pair transfer is the useful description, including cases without proton transfer. A correct label is not enough: point to the changed bond, proton, or electron pair.

Evidence boundary

Source Note
claims claim-bronsted-model, claim-lewis-modelsources source-openstax-chemistry, source-iupac-gold-book

The synthesis restates the two sourced definitions; consult the claims ledger for scope, uncertainty, and review status.

Accessibility and alternatives

The diagram source and SVG are not required to understand the lesson. Their full relationships are stated in the directive long descriptions and repeated in prose. Chemical equations are also described in words. No color, animation, audio, dragging, hover, or timed interaction carries unique information.

Callout

If symbolic formulas are difficult to parse, rewrite each species as a name and mark “gains one proton” or “loses one proton.” A text-only response earns the same credit as a diagram.

Recovery route

If donor and acceptor are reversed, return to one before-and-after formula pair and circle the species that gains a proton. If the Lewis model is unclear, review lone pairs and formal charge before retrying one untimed item. Do not repeat the full set until you can explain the error.

Callout

Retry item-conjugate-pair after explaining one prior error aloud or in writing. Unlimited attempts are allowed; use a different reaction on each retry.

items.prompt: In NH3 + H2O ⇌ NH4+ + OH-, which pair is a conjugate acid–base pair?

answer.summary: NH3 and NH4+ differ by one proton.
answer.reasoning: Ammonia accepts a proton to become ammonium, so ammonia is the base and ammonium is its conjugate acid.
commonErrors: Pairing species because they appear on opposite sides rather than because they differ by one proton.

items.prompt: Explain why BF3 + NH3 can be described as a Lewis acid–base reaction even though no proton is transferred.

answer.correct: NH3 donates a lone pair and BF3 accepts that electron pair.
answer.summary: The Lewis model tracks an electron-pair donation and acceptance.
answer.reasoning: Nitrogen in ammonia has an available lone pair, while electron-deficient boron in BF3 can accept it to form a coordinate bond.
acceptableResponses: Any response that identifies NH3 as electron-pair donor and BF3 as electron-pair acceptor.

items.prompt: A closed model contains a weak acid HA and its conjugate base A-. A small amount of strong acid is added. State which component consumes most of the added proton, explain the pH consequence, and state one limit of the model.

answer.correct: A- consumes most of the added proton to form HA, so pH changes far less than it would in unbuffered water; the resistance is finite and fails as buffer capacity is approached.
answer.summary: The conjugate base absorbs added proton, limiting but not preventing pH change.
answer.reasoning: Adding H+ shifts the model toward the weak acid. The HA/A- ratio changes, so pH moves slightly; once A- is largely consumed the buffer no longer resists further addition.
acceptableResponses: Any response naming A- as the proton acceptor, describing a small rather than zero pH change, and stating a limit such as finite capacity, closed-system idealization, or neglected activity effects.
commonErrors: Claiming a buffer holds pH exactly constant
Naming HA as the species that consumes added acid

criteria.description: Identifies donor and acceptor roles correctly.
Names the proton or electron pair whose transfer justifies the model.
Identifies the conjugate base as the component consuming added proton.
States that pH change is reduced rather than eliminated and names one boundary of the model.

agentAccess.notes: The current source page prohibits ingestion into generative-AI offerings. Contributor agents must not open or process its substantive content; a human rights and academic reviewer must verify any retained citation.

agentAccess.notes: Agent-ingestion permission was not established for this specimen, so contributor agents must treat the source as human-verification only.

claims.statement: In the Brønsted–Lowry model, an acid donates a proton and a base accepts a proton.
claims.scope: Introductory aqueous and molecular acid-base classification.

claims.statement: In the Lewis model, a base donates an electron pair and an acid accepts an electron pair.
claims.uncertainty: The simple donor-acceptor model does not by itself quantify reaction extent or strength.

claims.statement: A conjugate base component can consume a small added amount of proton, limiting the resulting pH change until buffer capacity is approached.
claims.scope: Simplified equilibrium model for a weak-acid/conjugate-base buffer.
claims.uncertainty: The statement does not specify biological compartments, activity corrections, or patient interpretation.

terms.definition: Two species related by the gain or loss of one proton.
An electron-pair donor in the Lewis acid–base model.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

This entire directory is a non-production format specimen. It does not represent a contributed, reviewed, adjudicated, or published EmbeddedKnowledge lesson and must never count toward course coverage.

The specimen prose, assessment, declarative diagram, and SVG were created for EmbeddedKnowledge and are offered under CC BY 4.0. No third-party media is redistributed. The source registry demonstrates claim provenance; a source listing does not imply that source text or artwork was copied into the specimen.

The OpenStax source entry is a reference, not an adaptation license determination. Editors must verify rights for the exact title, edition, format, and use before adapting material.

Both registry entries record a real, checkable licence rather than a hedge, because that is the behaviour the rights ledger is meant to model:

  • OpenStax Chemistry 2e — CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, retrieved 2026-07-19. Its NonCommercial and ShareAlike terms are incompatible with this CC BY 4.0 corpus, so the specimen cites and links it and copies nothing from it.
  • IUPAC Compendium of Chemical Terminology (Gold Book) — CC BY-SA 4.0 for individual terms, retrieved 2026-07-19. ShareAlike is likewise incompatible with CC BY 4.0 reuse, so term definitions are cited, never copied.

Each entry also carries an archivedUrl snapshot so the licence claim stays checkable after the publisher's page changes.

The v2 source registry separately records how each work is used and whether an agent may process it. Both sources are facts-only references: no source prose, organization, examples, questions, or media are reused. OpenStax's current page expressly restricts generative-AI ingestion, and agent permission for the IUPAC source was not established, so both are marked human-only for future contributor and reviewer agents.

The specimen declares two outcomes — topic-acids-bases-acid-base-models and topic-acids-bases-buffers — because the transfer scene and claim-buffer-response teach buffer response, which is a separate atomic outcome. Its graph prerequisite topic-acids-bases-weak-acid-base is therefore declared alongside topic-bonding-molecular-structure-lewis-formal-charge. Coverage is computed from declared outcomes, so a pack must declare everything it actually teaches and every prerequisite that teaching assumes.

aiAssistance.disclosure: This non-production specimen was drafted to exercise the format contract. It has not passed lesson review or adjudication.
